Meaning to convey:
Convey this: You have finite resources, and finite time to figure it out. Hiring decisions for who is to be the visionary in your AI product quest may become fatal mistakes. So I urge you to choose designers, product managers, and developers, who are linguists. Even if they don’t claim to be, because of course that is what everyone is going to say in the interview. Appoint a clear leader if you must, or a fully balanced collaborative leadership between a designer and a developer. Or a product manager with real experience actually launching an AI product that meaningfully impacted the business. The designer must a linguist, there is no debate. I often get asked by fellow designers if designer will get cut out by AI, and the truth is probably somewhere in the middle, that designers who don’t take a deeper interest in language, or some type of artistic form of expression in it’s place, will be the first to fall. I’m not talking about memorizing the parts of speech. Okay? Spare us. I’m talking about the original thinkers among you, and I’ve met oh so few.

Convey this: To caveat, there may be other workflow subtypes, but these three capture the majority of all products. The first workflow archetype is the the informational workflow, which involves the transference of data. Second is the transactional workflow, which entails the transference or exchange of goods or services. Last but not least is the creational workflow, or the “build workflow” as I like to call it, where components are transfered onto a “build”, and this phenomenon is at play in any form of creativity. The dileations between them are derived from the particular categories of values that they transfer: data, goods, components.

Convey this: ALT: Imagine you’re looking at a beautiful, intricate quilt. Each patch on the quilt could represent a small part of an image, think of these patches not as single, isolated pixels but rather as small but meaningful pieces of the picture. Each patch contains enough detail to be interesting or significant on its own, like a piece showing part of a flower or a bit of the sky. In the context of Transformers processing images, these patches are like those important snippets of the image. The Transformer looks at each patch to understand what it shows and how it relates to the patches around it. By examining all these patches together, seeing which colors and textures appear, how they’re arranged, and what they combine to form, the Transformer can grasp the overall picture and what it represents. So, in this quilt analogy, each patch is not just a raw, meaningless pixel. it’s a salient pattern, a meaningful piece of the larger image that the Transformer uses to build a complete understanding of what it’s looking at. This method allows it to see the inidividual pieces and the full picture and understand the details and the whole scene effectively.

Convey this: This raw state of information is significant, because it also describes how data is suspended in databases prior to being summoned. In databases, data is typically stored in its raw or unprocessed form, often referred to as “data at rest.” When a specific piece of data is needed or becomes relevant for a particular operation or query, it is called or summoned from the database. In the context of large language models like Transformers, the vast corpus of text data exists in a raw and relatively dormant state prior to being accessed or “called” by the model. This raw data comprises a multitude of linguistic patterns, structures, and information, but it lacks meaning or significance until it is processed and utilized by the model in response to a specific query or prompt, forming rich contexts and generating meaningful responses.

ALT: Imagine you’re looking at a beautiful, intricate quilt. Each patch on the quilt could represent a small part of an image—think of these patches not as single, isolated pixels but rather as small but meaningful pieces of the picture. Each patch contains enough detail to be interesting or significant on its own—like a piece

showing part of a flower or a bit of the sky. In the context of Transformers processing images, these patches are like those important snippets of the image. The Transformer looks at each patch to understand what it shows and how it relates to the patches around it. By examining all these patches together—seeing which colors and textures appear, how they’re arranged, and what they combine to form—the Transformer can grasp the overall picture and what it represents. So, in this quilt analogy, each patch is not just a raw, meaningless pixel; it’s a salient pattern, a meaningful piece of the larger image that the Transformer uses to build a complete understanding of what it’s looking at. This method allows it to see the inidividual pieces and the full picture and understand the details and the whole scene effectively.

concept of “what it is like to be a bat” comes from philosopher Thomas Nagel, who discussed it in his influential 1974 paper “What Is It Like to Be a Bat?” Nagel argued that consciousness has a subjective character, which means there is something it feels like to be a particular being. He used the example of a bat to illustrate how difficult it is to understand the subjective experience of another species, especially one so different from humans. Nagel’s work critiques reductionist approaches to consciousness, emphasizing that subjective experience cannot be fully explained by physical or functionalist theories.

Convey this: This engine is what the Transformer in GPT is. The term “transforming” refers to the process of converting input data (such as sequences of text) into meaningful representations that capture the relationships and patterns within that data. Meaning to convey:
Convey this: Language is fundamentally a rich tapestry where meaning emerges from the intricate interweaving of relationships between concepts, rather than from isolated words or ideas. These relationships manifest in myriad ways: causal relationships show how one event triggers another (‘the rain caused the flood’), temporal relationships establish sequence (‘dinner before dessert’), hierarchical relationships define categories (‘a sparrow is a bird’), entity relationships express possession or composition (‘a car has wheels’, ‘the wheel is part of the car’), enabling relationships show how one thing makes another possible (‘having a ticket enables entry’), attributive relationships describe properties (‘the sky is blue’), and contextual relationships reveal situational meaning (‘I’m fine’ can express either genuine contentment or subtle distress depending on the context). For artificial intelligence to truly grasp language as humans do, it must mirror this deep interconnected nature, learning not just individual patterns but the complex web of dependencies that give language its true expressive power.

Language is fundamentally a rich tapestry where meaning emerges from the intricate interweaving of relationships between concepts, rather than from isolated words or ideas. These relationships manifest in myriad ways: causal relationships show how one event triggers another (‘the rain caused the flood’), temporal relationships establish sequence (‘dinner before dessert’), hierarchical relationships define categories (‘a sparrow is a

bird’), entity relationships express possession or composition (‘a car has wheels’, ‘the wheel is part of the car’), enabling relationships show how one thing makes another possible (‘having a ticket enables entry’), attributive relationships describe properties (‘the sky is blue’), and contextual relationships reveal situational meaning (‘I’m fine’ can express either genuine contentment or subtle distress depending on the context). For artificial intelligence to truly grasp language as humans do, it must mirror this deep interconnected nature, learning not just individual patterns but the complex web of dependencies that give language its true expressive power.

Convey this: In design, things are programmable via states. And interconnected states we might call a scene. If I type something into the prompt bar, and make a selection, how can I get that change to reflect elsewhere in the UI? Via state changes to a component or organism. For instance, in Airblox, you can generate a cargo plan for a certain lane. If the user accepts the generated cargo plan, how is that action taken within the AI chat interface reflected on the left side of the screen? By making the organism on the left side progress into a state change that adds a callout for the “awarded” carriers from within thr AI card. So you can see, the result of an AI outcome protocol action is merely changing to a component variant from your design system. This is where we have the semblance of a Fluid UI beginning to form. The semi-monolithic UI appears to have some generative elements, but the reality is that the illusory magic trick is merely contextual awareness of the current UI scene and the utilization of atomic state changes to components, using organism variants essentially.

The core connection between non-verbal Transformers and words lies in their shared reliance on recognizing and interpreting patterns of value, where “patterns” signify meaningful relationships within data, and value is both the meaningless raw data and the meaning of the pattern. Whether parsing sentences, discerning shapes in an image, or decoding sequences in audio, Transformers identify and utilize these patterns to derive context and insight. This unified approach to different data types hinges on the Transformer’s ability to treat all data—words, image patches, or sound segments—as sequences of input that can be analyzed for underlying structure and relationships. By applying this principle across modalities, Transformers transcend the traditional boundaries of language processing, leveraging pattern recognition to bring coherence and understanding to diverse forms of information.]

non-verbal gestural communication, to music. Even outside of humanity, trees use patterns to communicate. Each of these represents a semantic subdomain, the language of which is defined by the atomic patterns that comprise the builds. Large-language-models thus can wield pattern at a fundamental level, prior to spoken language, capable in every semantic subdomain. Within the realm of AI, models are trained to understand these building blocks to wield as the material of the generative engines. This is why as Raskin said, you can treat everything as language, because at the fundamental level AI is pattern recognition, just as at the fundamental level, language is pattern and pattern is language.

This raw state of information is significant, because it also describes how data is suspended in databases prior to being summoned. In databases, data is typically stored in its raw or unprocessed form, often referred to as “data at rest.” When a specific piece of data is needed or becomes relevant for a particular operation

or query, it is called or summoned from the database. In the context of large language models like Transformers, the vast corpus of text data exists in a raw and relatively dormant state prior to being accessed or “called” by the model. This raw data comprises a multitude of linguistic patterns, structures, and information, but it lacks meaning or significance until it is processed and utilized by the model in response to a specific query or prompt, forming rich contexts and generating meaningful responses.

My go-to example of the transference of value in human-machine interaction is a pilot (human) in an aircraft cockpit (machine). In a cockpit, a pilot is enveloped within an avionic dashboard of switches, gauges, and data visualizations that tell the story of what is going on “out

there”. It conveys to the pilot everything from the plane’s altitude and airspeed, to its tilt and engine thrust. This data serves as a comprehensive communication system, metaphorically relaying the aircraft’s status to the pilot. Upon studying this information, the pilot can then employ gestural commands such as turning the yoke – avionics for steering wheel – in the desired direction to express directional commands. In response to the pilot’s guidance, the aircraft executes these commands, and with adjusted dashboard data, the pilot is prompted to evaluate the maneuver. And so the conversation between the pilot and the aircraft continues in this constant feedback loop, where informational and command values are being transferred from one entity to the other, and the conversation is a dance in the sky.

Convey this: My go-to example of the transference of value in human-machine interaction is a pilot (human) in an aircraft cockpit (machine). In a cockpit, a pilot is enveloped within an avionic dashboard of switches, gauges, and data visualizations that tell the story of what is going on “out there”. It conveys to the pilot everything from the plane’s altitude and airspeed, to its tilt and engine thrust. This data serves as a comprehensive communication system, metaphorically relaying the aircraft’s status to the pilot. Upon studying this information, the pilot can then employ gestural commands such as turning the yoke – avionics for steering wheel – in the desired direction to express directional commands. In response to the pilot’s guidance, the aircraft executes these commands, and with adjusted dashboard data, the pilot is prompted to evaluate the maneuver. And so the conversation between the pilot and the aircraft continues in this constant feedback loop, where informational and command values are being transferred from one entity to the other, and the conversation is a dance in the sky.
